images.caption:
Figure 1:Per-class quality lift under 3-judge audit.Learned routing improves most strongly on coordination-heavy
classes, especially C3 cross-document multi-vendor reasoning, C7
mitigation correctness, and C8 cross-vendor pair tasks. Procedural
classes are ties or narrow trades.

Figure 2:AgensFlow at a glance.Left: the per-task
lifecycle (steps 1 to 6), from pre-flight gating through task
feature computation, regime detection, UCB1-based action
selection over the variant pool, execution, and trajectory
scoring with policy-graph backup. Right: the framework’s
persistent components and how they interact with the
lifecycle: the policy graph that UCB1 consults, the router
that dispatches actions,RelativeJudgewith
its hybrid reward and audit hooks. Note that thesix stepson the left are a procedural per-task flow;
they should not be confused with thesix dependency
layersof the framework (Table1below), which
are a structural decomposition where every layer is active
simultaneously at runtime.

Figure 3:Cold-start learning trajectory.The main substrate initially compresses cost asskip:Xusage rises,
then rebalances after quality drops. The pattern supports that skip decisions are being adjusted by reward feedback rather than fixed by the initial pipeline.

Figure 4:Warm-start transfer.Warm-starting from the
distributed-systems policy graph reduces early exploration cost on the
synthetic security-advisory corpus while preserving plateau quality
under cross-family audit. The figure also shows why the single-judge
result requires audit: quality differences are modest under 3-judge
scoring, while token compression is judge-independent.
